schools.summary: Chrysostom: the whole passage is obscure, the obscurity produced by our ignorance of the facts referred to and by their cessation — the miraculous gifts that then used to occur but now no longer take place; Paul taking up the subject of spiritual gifts, desiring the Corinthians not be ignorant — the introduction to the great teaching on the gifts of the Spirit (the extraordinary endowments, tongues, prophecy, healing, miracles, of the apostolic age, which the Corinthians prized and abused, coveting the showy and despising the lowly, dividing over them), which Paul will set in order (showing their source, diversity, purpose, and the supremacy of love).

themes: concerning spiritual gifts
I would not have you ignorant
the extraordinary endowments
the gifts to be set in order

schools.summary: Gill: Paul reminds them they were Gentiles (by religion, profane persons given to wickedness, bigotry, and superstition) — said to humble them, by putting them in mind of their former state; Paul reminding the Corinthians that they were once Gentiles, carried away to dumb (lifeless, speechless) idols as they were led (blindly, by their priests, customs, and the devil) — the humbling recollection of the grace that delivered them (that they, now endowed with spiritual gifts, were once enslaved to dumb idols), the contrast of the dumb idols with the living Spirit who now speaks through them, the magnifying of the grace of deliverance.

themes: ye were Gentiles
carried away unto these dumb idols
even as ye were led
the humbling reminder of grace

schools.summary: Barnes: the force is to give a rule by which they may know what influences and operations are from God — a simple general guide to distinguish the operations of the Spirit; Paul giving the test — no one speaking by the Spirit of God calls Jesus accursed, and no one can truly say Jesus is Lord but by the Holy Ghost — the touchstone of the Spirit's working (the true Spirit always honours Christ, none speaking by him cursing Jesus, and the sincere confession of Jesus as Lord, with the heart not merely the lips, being itself the Spirit's work, none able so to confess but by the Spirit), the test distinguishing the true Spirit from the false by whether Christ is honoured.

debates.question: What does 'none can say Jesus is Lord but by the Holy Ghost' mean?

sides.position: The sincere, heartfelt confession of Jesus as Lord (not merely the words) is the Spirit's work; the true Spirit always honours Christ — the test distinguishing the true Spirit from the false

themes: no man speaking by the Spirit calleth Jesus accursed
none can say Jesus is Lord but by the Holy Ghost
the test of the Spirit
the true Spirit honours Christ

schools.summary: Gill: the gifts spring from the free grace, and good will and pleasure of God, and are not owing to the merits of men — so those with the largest share should not boast as if acquired by themselves, and those with the smallest should be content and thankful; Paul declaring that there are diversities of gifts, but the same Spirit — the unity in diversity (the various spiritual gifts, however different, all proceeding from the one and the same Spirit), so that no gift is to be despised (all being the Spirit's, even the lowliest) and none made a ground of pride (all being of grace, not one's own).

themes: there are diversities of gifts
but the same Spirit
the unity in diversity
none to be despised or boasted

schools.summary: Gill: 'differences of administrations' are the different offices in the church — apostles, prophets, pastors/teachers, deacons — ministered by different persons, employed in planting and forming churches, ordaining elders, preaching the word, administering ordinances, and caring for the poor; Paul declaring that there are differences of administrations (ministries, offices) but the same Lord — the diversity of services under the one Lord Jesus Christ (who appoints and is served by them all), the parallel with the gifts (v.4, diverse but from one Spirit; here diverse but under one Lord), the Trinitarian distinction (the Lord Christ distinguished from the Spirit, v.4, and God, v.6).

themes: there are differences of administrations
but the same Lord
the various ministries under one Lord
the Trinitarian distinction

schools.summary: Gill: there are operations ordinary (the good work of grace in all the elect — faith, love, hope) and extraordinary (here meant, the miraculous workings hereafter specified); Paul declaring that there are diversities of operations (workings, effects) but it is the same God which works all in all — the completion of the Trinitarian pattern (the gifts of the Spirit, v.4, the ministries of the Lord, v.5, and the operations of God, v.6, all the various effects proceeding from the one Triune God), the unity of the Triune source (Spirit, Lord, and God), the diversity grounded in the one God who distributes and works all in all as he wills.

themes: there are diversities of operations
the same God which worketh all in all
the Trinitarian pattern of the gifts
the one God the source

schools.summary: Gill: 'the manifestation of the Spirit' is the gifts of the Spirit, which manifest that a man has the Spirit (the fruits and graces of the Spirit, even the least measure showing it), given to every man (who has them) to profit withal; Paul declaring that the manifestation of the Spirit is given to every man to profit withal — the purpose of the gifts (given not for private display, pride, or gain, but for the common profit, the edification of the whole church, to serve and build up others), the corrective to the Corinthian abuse (who used the gifts for self-display, pride, and division).

themes: the manifestation of the Spirit
is given to every man to profit withal
the gifts for the common good
not for private display or pride

schools.summary: Gill: Paul now gives a distinct and particular enumeration of the operations of the Spirit, the manifestations that most clearly show him to be the author to different persons; the word of wisdom and the word of knowledge among the first; to one the word of wisdom (the deeper insight into the gospel mysteries, or the wise application of the truth to cases and consciences), to another the word of knowledge (the clear understanding and explanation of the doctrines, the knowledge of the Scriptures and the truth) — both gifts of utterance (for teaching, expounding, applying the truth), given by the one and the same Spirit, even within the gifts of utterance a diversity.

themes: to one is given... the word of wisdom
to another the word of knowledge
the gifts of utterance
both by the one Spirit

schools.summary: Gill: the 'faith' here is not the saving grace common to all believers (though given by the same Spirit) but a special faith — the faith of miracles, or boldness to assert and defend the doctrine of faith; to another faith, to another the gifts of healing, by the same Spirit — the diversity of the miraculous gifts (a special faith: the faith of miracles, the confidence to do wonders, as moving mountains, 1 Corinthians 13:2, or extraordinary boldness in confessing and defending the truth; and the gifts of healing: the power to cure diseases) — supernatural endowments of the apostolic age, both by the one Spirit.

debates.question: What is the 'faith' that is a spiritual gift here?

sides.position: Not saving faith (common to all believers) but a special gift — the faith of miracles (to do wonders) or extraordinary boldness in confessing and defending the truth

themes: to another faith by the same Spirit
to another the gifts of healing
the faith of miracles
the miraculous gifts

schools.summary: Gill: 'the working of miracles' (powers) are mighty deeds apparently above and out of the reach of nature — raising the dead, causing the blind to see, the deaf to hear, the dumb to speak, the lame to walk; Paul completing the list — the working of miracles, prophecy (foretelling or expounding by inspiration), discerning of spirits (distinguishing the true Spirit/prophet from the false), divers kinds of tongues (speaking languages not learned), and the interpretation of tongues (rendering them into the known language) — the full range of the extraordinary gifts distributed among the members (each his own, none all), tongues and interpretation paired (the tongue useless without the interpretation, 1 Corinthians 14).

themes: the working of miracles; to another prophecy
discerning of spirits
divers kinds of tongues... interpretation
the full range of the extraordinary gifts

schools.summary: Gill: though the gifts are so different and bestowed on different persons, they are all wrought by one and the same Spirit of God, who is the true Jehovah and properly God — for who but the most high God could ever communicate such gifts to men?; Paul declaring that all these gifts are wrought by one and the same Spirit, who divides to every man severally as he wills — the deity of the Spirit (that he works all these gifts proving him God, only God able so to endow men) and his sovereign distribution (he distributes the gifts as he wills, not as men merit or choose), correcting both envy (none should envy another's gift) and pride (none should boast, the Spirit gave it as he willed).

debates.question: What does the Spirit's working all the gifts prove?

sides.position: His deity — only the most high God could communicate such gifts; he is the true Jehovah, distributing sovereignly 'as he will'

themes: all these worketh that one and the selfsame Spirit
dividing to every man severally as he will
the deity of the Spirit
the sovereign distribution

schools.summary: Chrysostom: having soothed them (the gifts being of free favour, from one and the same Spirit, given to profit, even the lesser gifts a manifestation) and stopped their mouths from the duty of yielding to the Spirit's authority, Paul now uses the body to teach the concord of the many in one; the body is one with many members, yet one body, so also is Christ — the great image of the church as the body of Christ (as the natural body is one though composed of many members, so the church is one though composed of many members with diverse gifts, united in Christ the Head), 'so also is Christ' meaning the church so closely united to him that it bears his name.

themes: as the body is one, and hath many members
being many, are one body
so also is Christ
the church as Christ's body

schools.summary: Gill: this is not water baptism (which does not incorporate into the body, and which all the baptized do not have the Spirit, as Simon Magus), but the baptism of the Spirit — the regenerating and uniting work; Paul declaring that by one Spirit all are baptized into one body, whether Jews or Gentiles, bond or free, and all made to drink into one Spirit — the Spirit's work of uniting all believers into the one body of Christ regardless of nation or status (no distinction), the baptism (incorporating into the body) and the drinking (the indwelling, partaking of the Spirit) together signifying the Spirit's uniting and indwelling work.

debates.question: Is 'baptized by one Spirit into one body' water baptism or Spirit baptism?

sides.position: The baptism of the Spirit (the regenerating, uniting work) — not water baptism, which does not incorporate into the body and which not all the baptized truly share

themes: by one Spirit are we all baptized into one body
whether Jews or Gentiles, bond or free
made to drink into one Spirit
the Spirit uniting all into Christ's body

schools.summary: Gill: the church of Christ is not one person only, nor consists of one sort of persons — not only of Jews, or only of Gentiles, or only of the rich and freemen, or only of men of extraordinary gifts and abilities, or greatly eminent for grace; Clarke: the body is not one member; Paul stating that the body is not one member, but many — the foundational truth (the church, like the body, is composed not of one member or sort but of many, with diverse gifts and offices, so that uniformity is neither possible nor desirable, the diversity essential to the body's life), setting up the argument (vv.15-26, that each member, however lowly, is needed, and none may despise or be despised).

themes: the body is not one member, but many
the church not of one sort
the diversity essential to its life
the many members

schools.summary: Gill: the foot is the lowest member of the body, nearest the earth, treading on it, sustaining the whole weight, performing the more drudging and fatiguing exercises — representing one in the lowest station in the church (a doorkeeper in the house of God, the least of saints, who thinks himself so); Paul putting the false reasoning of the lowly member — if the foot says 'because I am not the hand, I am not of the body,' it is not therefore excluded from the body — the rebuke of false humility and discontent (the lowly member, the believer of small gifts, must not, out of envy or discontent, reason himself out of the body, for his lesser place/function does not unmake him a member).

themes: if the foot shall say, Because I am not the hand
I am not of the body
is it therefore not of the body?
the rebuke of discontent

schools.summary: Gill: the ear (the organ that receives sounds) may design those who hear the word of God internally — who hear so as to love, savour, understand, and believe it, and distinguish it for themselves; Paul repeating the argument with the ear — if the ear says 'because I am not the eye, I am not of the body,' it is not therefore excluded — the further rebuke of discontent (the member of one function, the ear, must not, because it is not another, the eye, the more honoured, reason itself out of the body, for the diversity of function does not unmake the member), the wrong comparison to the more honoured (each member needed in its own function).

themes: if the ear shall say, Because I am not the eye
I am not of the body
is it therefore not of the body?
the rebuke of discontent over diverse function

schools.summary: Barnes: the idea is that all the parts of the body are useful in their proper place, and it would be as absurd to require or expect that all the members of the church should have the same endowments as to attempt to make the body all eye; Paul showing the absurdity — if the whole body were an eye, where would the hearing be? if all hearing, where the smelling? — the demonstration that uniformity would destroy the body (if all members were the same, all eye or all ear, the body would lose its other functions and cease to be a body), the diversity of members necessary (each function needed for the body's life), applied to the church (uniformity of gifts would destroy it).

themes: if the whole body were an eye, where were the hearing?
if the whole were hearing, where were the smelling?
uniformity would destroy the body
the diversity necessary

schools.summary: Gill: the members of the natural body, as they are all made and fashioned by God in the form they have, so they are each set by him in the place they are; Barnes: God has formed the body, with its various members, as he saw would best conduce to the harmony and usefulness of all; Paul declaring that God has set the members, every one, in the body as it pleased him — the sovereign wisdom of God in the body's design (each member having its place and function by God's appointment and good pleasure, none by chance, all by the wise Creator), so that none may complain of his place or envy another's (all set by God's good pleasure for the good of the whole).

themes: God hath set the members every one... in the body
as it hath pleased him
the sovereign wisdom in the design
none to complain or envy

schools.summary: Barnes: if there were nothing but an eye, an ear, or a limb, there would be no body — and if there were not variety of talent and endowment in the church, the church could not itself exist; Paul asking, if all were one member, where would the body be? — the demonstration that the many members are necessary to the body (a single member is not a body, and without the variety of members, and in the church of gifts, there would be no body at all), the diversity essential (without it, no body, no church).

themes: if they were all one member
where were the body?
the many members necessary
without variety, no body

schools.summary: Gill: the members are many, of different make and shape, in different parts and places, and of different use and service; Ellicott: Paul turns from the reductio ad absurdum of the previous verses to the fact as it is, proceeding (v.21) to the mutual interdependence of the members; Paul stating that there are many members yet but one body — the balance of diversity and unity (the members many, diverse in form, place, and function, yet forming but one body, neither uniformity, all one member, v.19, nor division), applied to the church (many members/diverse gifts yet one body, united in Christ).

themes: now are they many members
yet but one body
the balance of diversity and unity
the diverse members forming one body

schools.summary: Chrysostom: having checked the envy of those in lower rank, and taken off their dejection, Paul now humbles the pride of those who had received the greater gifts — the statement that it was a gift and not an achievement abasing them; Paul declaring that the eye cannot say to the hand, nor the head to the feet, 'I have no need of thee' — the rebuke of the pride of the higher members (the more honoured, the eye, the head, may not despise or dispense with the lower, the hand, the feet, for even the highest needs the lowest, the body's interdependence forbidding all contempt), the higher gift being a gift not an achievement (no ground for pride).

themes: the eye cannot say unto the hand, I have no need of thee
nor the head to the feet
the rebuke of the higher members' pride
the body's interdependence

schools.summary: Gill: Paul proceeds, in a beautiful gradation, to take notice of the parts of the body that are more weak, dishonourable, and uncomely, showing their necessity and usefulness; Barnes: the feeble members are weaker than the rest, less able to bear fatigue, more easily injured; Paul declaring that, much more, the members that seem more feeble are necessary — the surpassing necessity of the weak members (the parts that seem weaker, the internal organs, the hidden vital parts as the heart and eyes, are not only useful but necessary, often more so than the strong limbs), so that the weak in the church, the lowly and less gifted, are far from dispensable (often more necessary than the prominent).

themes: those members... which seem to be more feeble
are necessary
the weak often more vital
the weak in the church not dispensable

schools.summary: Barnes: 'more abundant honour' refers to clothing — we bestow more attention and honour on the parts not naturally comely than on the face that is deemed comely (covered and adorned), and adorn the uncomely parts; Paul observing that the members thought less honourable receive more abundant honour, and the uncomely parts more abundant comeliness — the principle that nature itself teaches honour to the lowly (the less honourable and uncomely parts, covered, adorned, and protected, receive the greater care and honour), so that in the church the lowly members, the less gifted and obscure, should be specially honoured (as nature honours the less honourable parts).

themes: less honourable... we bestow more abundant honour
our uncomely parts have more abundant comeliness
nature teaches honour to the lowly
the lowly members specially honoured

schools.summary: Gill: the comely parts (the face, eyes, etc.) have no need of an external covering or ornament; so such as are blessed with gifts and eminent for grace need no outward adorning; Paul declaring that the comely parts have no need, but God has tempered (blended, fitly joined) the body together, giving more abundant honour to the part that lacked — the divine design that honours the lowly (the comely parts, sufficient in themselves, needing no added honour, but God so blending the body that the deficient/less comely parts receive the greater honour, His arrangement deliberately exalting the lowly), God's wisdom and equity (the lowly compensated, all cared for, the body knit together).

themes: our comely parts have no need
God hath tempered the body together
more abundant honour to that part which lacked
the divine design honouring the lowly

schools.summary: Gill: the design is that there be no schism in the body — no complaint of one member against another as useless and unnecessary, no murmuring, no tumults, no dissension, no division; Paul declaring that God so tempered the body that there should be no schism, but that the members should have the same care one for another — the purpose of the divine design (that, by honouring the lowly and knitting all together, v.24, God prevents the division that pride and contempt would cause and produces mutual care, each member caring for the others as for itself), applied to the church (no schism but mutual care, the members caring for one another).

themes: that there should be no schism in the body
the members should have the same care one for another
the purpose of the design
the mutual care

schools.summary: Barnes: this we all know is the case with the body — a pain in the foot, the hand, or the head excites deep solicitude, the interest not confined to the part affected; Paul declaring that if one member suffers, all the members suffer with it, and if one is honoured, all rejoice with it — the sympathy of the body (the members sharing each other's pain and joy, bound together in fellow-feeling), applied to the church (the believers suffering with the afflicted and rejoicing with the honoured, Romans 12:15, without envy, bound together in sympathy and love).

themes: one member suffer, all the members suffer with it
one member be honoured, all the members rejoice
the body's fellow-feeling
the sympathy applied to the church

schools.summary: Chrysostom: lest any say the body is a slave to nature but our good deeds are of choice, Paul applies the figure to our own concerns — that we ought to have the same concord of design (by choice) as the body has from nature; 'now ye are the body of Christ'; Paul applying the whole figure — the Corinthians are the body of Christ, and members in particular (each individually a member) — the direct application of the body-image to the church (the believers collectively Christ's body, each individually a member with his own place and gift), so that all the foregoing, the diversity, interdependence, mutual care, and honour to the lowly, applies to them (united in design and love by choice).

themes: now ye are the body of Christ
and members in particular
the figure applied to the church
each a member with his own place

schools.summary: Gill: having described the various gifts of the Spirit, the apostle now enumerates the several offices and officers God has appointed in the church; Lightfoot notes the order — first apostles, second prophets, third teachers; Paul listing the offices and gifts God has set in the church — first apostles, then prophets, teachers, miracles, gifts of healings, helps (assisting), governments (ruling), diversities of tongues — the divine order and ranking of ministries (God himself appointing the offices in a definite order, the apostles first as foundational, tongues last), the most prized gift (tongues, which the Corinthians prized) ranked lowest (teaching that the most showy is not the highest), all of God's setting (none self-assumed).

themes: God hath set some in the church, first apostles
prophets... teachers... miracles... helps, governments... tongues
the divine order of ministries
the most prized gift ranked lowest

schools.summary: Barnes: these questions imply, with strong emphasis, that it could not be and ought not to be that there should be perfect equality of endowment — not all were equal, or all equally qualified; Paul asking, by a series of questions, whether all are apostles, prophets, teachers, or workers of miracles — the demonstration of the necessary diversity (as in the body not every member is the eye or the hand, so in the church not all hold the same office or have the same gift, the questions expecting the answer 'no'), rebuking the desire for uniformity (the Corinthians' wish that all have the prized gifts), the diversity by divine distribution (God set the offices and gifts, v.28).

themes: are all apostles? are all prophets?
are all teachers? are all workers of miracles?
not all have the same gift
the rebuke of uniformity

schools.summary: Gill: when these gifts were in being, all had them not — the gifts of healing belonged to the elders of the church (who anointed the sick with oil), not the common members; and not all spoke with or interpreted tongues; Lightfoot notes the gifts of healings, the speaking with tongues; Paul continuing the questions — have all the gifts of healing? do all speak with tongues? do all interpret? — the further demonstration that the gifts are diversely distributed (not all have healing, tongues, or interpretation), so that even the prized gift of tongues was never meant for all (not all speak with tongues), the variety by God's appointment (God set the gifts diversely, v.28).

themes: have all the gifts of healing?
do all speak with tongues? do all interpret?
the diverse distribution of the gifts
tongues never meant for all

schools.summary: Gill: this may be rendered as an assertion ('ye do covet earnestly the best gifts') or an exhortation; either way, Paul points to something better and more excellent than these — the way of love; Paul exhorting them to covet earnestly the best gifts (the more edifying, as prophecy over tongues, 1 Corinthians 14, the gifts that build up the church), yet promising to show a more excellent way — the transition to the great chapter on love (1 Corinthians 13), the way that surpasses all gifts (love above all, for the gifts profit nothing without it, 13:1-3, and it abides when they cease).

themes: covet earnestly the best gifts
yet shew I unto you a more excellent way
the transition to the chapter on love
the way of love surpassing all gifts
